title: Claude Certified Architect — Foundations: Study Guide

units.title: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution
subtopics: The agentic loop lifecycle: sending requests to Claude, inspecting stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn"), executing requested tools, and returning results for the next iteration
How tool results are appended to conversation history so the model can reason about the next action
The distinction between model-driven decision-making (Claude reasons about which tool to call next based on context) and pre-configured decision trees or tool sequences
Implementing agentic loop control flow that continues when stop_reason is "tool_use" and terminates when stop_reason is "end_turn"
Adding tool results to conversation context between iterations so the model can incorporate new information into its reasoning

Avoiding anti-patterns such as parsing natural language signals to determine loop termination, setting arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism, or checking for assistant text content as a completion indicator
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns
subtopics: Hub-and-spoke architecture where a coordinator agent manages all inter-subagent communication, error handling, and information routing
How subagents operate with isolated context—they do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history automatically
The role of the coordinator in task decomposition, delegation, result aggregation, and deciding which subagents to invoke based on query complexity
Risks of overly narrow task decomposition by the coordinator, leading to incomplete coverage of broad research topics
Designing coordinator agents that analyze query requirements and dynamically select which subagents to invoke rather than always routing through the full pipeline
Partitioning research scope across subagents to minimize duplication (e.g., assigning distinct subtopics or source types to each agent)

Implementing iterative refinement loops where the coordinator evaluates synthesis output for gaps, re-delegates to search and analysis subagents with targeted queries, and re-invokes synthesis until coverage is sufficient
Routing all subagent communication through the coordinator for observability, consistent error handling, and controlled information flow
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.3 Configure subagent invocation, context passing, and spawning
subtopics: The Task tool as the mechanism for spawning subagents, and the requirement that allowedTools must include "Task" for a coordinator to invoke subagents
That subagent context must be explicitly provided in the prompt—subagents do not automatically inherit parent context or share memory between invocations
The AgentDefinition configuration including descriptions, system prompts, and tool restrictions for each subagent type
Fork-based session management for exploring divergent approaches from a shared analysis baseline
Including complete findings from prior agents directly in the subagent's prompt (e.g., passing web search results and document analysis outputs to the synthesis subagent)
Using structured data formats to separate content from metadata (source URLs, document names, page numbers) when passing context between agents to preserve attribution
Spawning parallel subagents by emitting multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response rather than across separate turns
Designing coordinator prompts that specify research goals and quality criteria rather than step-by-step procedural instructions, to enable subagent adaptability
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.4 Implement multi-step workflows with enforcement and handoff patterns
subtopics: The difference between programmatic enforcement (hooks, prerequisite gates) and prompt-based guidance for workflow ordering
When deterministic compliance is required (e.g., identity verification before financial operations), prompt instructions alone have a non-zero failure rate
Structured handoff protocols for mid-process escalation that include customer details, root cause analysis, and recommended actions
Implementing programmatic prerequisites that block downstream tool calls until prerequisite steps have completed (e.g., blocking process_refund until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID)
Decomposing multi-concern customer requests into distinct items, then investigating each in parallel using shared context before synthesizing a unified resolution
Compiling structured handoff summaries (customer ID, root cause, refund amount, recommended action) when escalating to human agents who lack access to the conversation transcript
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.5 Apply Agent SDK hooks for tool call interception and data normalization
subtopics: Hook patterns (e.g., PostToolUse) that intercept tool results for transformation before the model processes them
Hook patterns that intercept outgoing tool calls to enforce compliance rules (e.g., blocking refunds above a threshold)
The distinction between using hooks for deterministic guarantees versus relying on prompt instructions for probabilistic compliance
Implementing PostToolUse hooks to normalize heterogeneous data formats (Unix timestamps, ISO 8601, numeric status codes) from different MCP tools before the agent processes them
Implementing tool call interception hooks that block policy-violating actions (e.g., refunds exceeding $500) and redirect to alternative workflows (e.g., human escalation)
Choosing hooks over prompt-based enforcement when business rules require guaranteed compliance
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.6 Design task decomposition strategies for complex workflows
subtopics: When to use fixed sequential pipelines (prompt chaining) versus dynamic adaptive decomposition based on intermediate findings
Prompt chaining patterns that break reviews into sequential steps (e.g., analyze each file individually, then run a cross-file integration pass)
The value of adaptive investigation plans that generate subtasks based on what is discovered at each step
Selecting task decomposition patterns appropriate to the workflow: prompt chaining for predictable multi-aspect reviews, dynamic decomposition for open-ended investigation tasks
Splitting large code reviews into per-file local analysis passes plus a separate cross-file integration pass to avoid attention dilution

Decomposing open-ended tasks (e.g., "add comprehensive tests to a legacy codebase") by first mapping structure, identifying high-impact areas, then creating a prioritized plan that adapts as dependencies are discovered
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 1.7 Manage session state, resumption, and forking
subtopics: Named session resumption using --resume <session-name> to continue a specific prior conversation
fork_session for creating independent branches from a shared analysis baseline to explore divergent approaches
The importance of informing the agent about changes to previously analyzed files when resuming sessions after code modifications
Why starting a new session with a structured summary is more reliable than resuming with stale tool results
Using --resume with session names to continue named investigation sessions across work sessions
Using fork_session to create parallel exploration branches (e.g., comparing two testing strategies or refactoring approaches from a shared codebase analysis)
Choosing between session resumption (when prior context is mostly valid) and starting fresh with injected summaries (when prior tool results are stale)
Informing a resumed session about specific file changes for targeted re-analysis rather than requiring full re-exploration
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.1 Design effective tool interfaces with clear descriptions and boundaries
subtopics: Tool descriptions as the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection; minimal descriptions lead to unreliable selection among similar tools
The importance of including input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundary explanations in tool descriptions
How ambiguous or overlapping tool descriptions cause misrouting (e.g., analyze_content vs analyze_document with near-identical descriptions)
The impact of system prompt wording on tool selection: keyword-sensitive instructions can create unintended tool associations
Writing tool descriptions that clearly differentiate each tool's purpose, expected inputs, outputs, and when to use it versus similar alternatives
Renaming tools and updating descriptions to eliminate functional overlap (e.g., renaming analyze_content to extract_web_results with a web-specific description)

Splitting generic tools into purpose-specific tools with defined input/output contracts (e.g., splitting a generic analyze_document into extract_data_points, summarize_content, and verify_claim_against_source)
Reviewing system prompts for keyword-sensitive instructions that might override well-written tool descriptions
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.2 Implement structured error responses for MCP tools
subtopics: The MCP isError flag pattern for communicating tool failures back to the agent
The distinction between transient errors (timeouts, service unavailability), validation errors (invalid input), business errors (policy violations), and permission errors
Why uniform error responses (generic "Operation failed") prevent the agent from making appropriate recovery decisions
The difference between retryable and non-retryable errors, and how returning structured metadata prevents wasted retry attempts
Returning structured error metadata including errorCategory (transient/validation/permission), isRetryable boolean, and human-readable descriptions
Including retriable: false flags and customer-friendly explanations for business rule violations so the agent can communicate appropriately
Implementing local error recovery within subagents for transient failures, propagating to the coordinator only errors that cannot be resolved locally along with partial results and what was attempted
Distinguishing between access failures (needing retry decisions) and valid empty results (representing successful queries with no matches)
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.3 Distribute tools appropriately across agents and configure tool choice
subtopics: The principle that giving an agent access to too many tools (e.g., 18 instead of 4-5) degrades tool selection reliability by increasing decision complexity
Why agents with tools outside their specialization tend to misuse them (e.g., a synthesis agent attempting web searches)
Scoped tool access: giving agents only the tools needed for their role, with limited cross-role tools for specific high-frequency needs
tool_choice configuration options: "auto", "any", and forced tool selection ({"type": "tool", "name": "..."})
Restricting each subagent's tool set to those relevant to its role, preventing cross-specialization misuse
Replacing generic tools with constrained alternatives (e.g., replacing fetch_url with load_document that validates document URLs)
Providing scoped cross-role tools for high-frequency needs (e.g., a verify_fact tool for the synthesis agent) while routing complex cases through the coordinator
Using tool_choice forced selection to ensure a specific tool is called first (e.g., forcing extract_metadata before enrichment tools), then processing subsequent steps in follow-up turns
Setting tool_choice: "any" to guarantee the model calls a tool rather than returning conversational text
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.4 Integrate MCP servers into Claude Code and agent workflows
subtopics: MCP server scoping: project-level (.mcp.json) for shared team tooling vs user-level (~/.claude.json) for personal/experimental servers
Environment variable expansion in .mcp.json (e.g., ${GITHUB_TOKEN}) for credential management without committing secrets
That tools from all configured MCP servers are discovered at connection time and available simultaneously to the agent
MCP resources as a mechanism for exposing content catalogs (e.g., issue summaries, documentation hierarchies, database schemas) to reduce exploratory tool calls
Configuring shared MCP servers in project-scoped .mcp.json with environment variable expansion for authentication tokens
Configuring personal/experimental MCP servers in user-scoped ~/.claude.json
Enhancing MCP tool descriptions to explain capabilities and outputs in detail, preventing the agent from preferring built-in tools (like Grep) over more capable MCP tools
Choosing existing community MCP servers over custom implementations for standard integrations (e.g., Jira), reserving custom servers for team-specific workflows
Exposing content catalogs as MCP resources to give agents visibility into available data without requiring exploratory tool calls
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 2.5 Select and apply built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob) effectively
subtopics: Grep for content search (searching file contents for patterns like function names, error messages, or import statements)
Glob for file path pattern matching (finding files by name or extension patterns)
Read/Write for full file operations; Edit for targeted modifications using unique text matching
When Edit fails due to non-unique text matches, using Read + Write as a fallback for reliable file modifications
Selecting Grep for searching code content across a codebase (e.g., finding all callers of a function, locating error messages)
Selecting Glob for finding files matching naming patterns (e.g., **/*.test.tsx)
Using Read to load full file contents followed by Write when Edit cannot find unique anchor text
Building codebase understanding incrementally: starting with Grep to find entry points, then using Read to follow imports and trace flows, rather than reading all files upfront
Tracing function usage across wrapper modules by first identifying all exported names, then searching for each name across the codebase
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.1 Configure CLAUDE.md files with appropriate hierarchy, scoping, and modular organization
subtopics: The CLAUDE.md configuration hierarchy: user-level (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), project-level (.claude/CLAUDE.md or root CLAUDE.md), and directory-level (subdirectory CLAUDE.md files)
That user-level settings apply only to that user—instructions in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md are not shared with teammates via version control
The @import syntax for referencing external files to keep CLAUDE.md modular (e.g., importing specific standards files relevant to each package)
.claude/rules/ directory for organizing topic-specific rule files as an alternative to a monolithic CLAUDE.md
Diagnosing configuration hierarchy issues (e.g., a new team member not receiving instructions because they're in user-level rather than project-level configuration)
Using @import to selectively include relevant standards files in each package's CLAUDE.md based on maintainer domain knowledge
Splitting large CLAUDE.md files into focused topic-specific files in .claude/rules/ (e.g., testing.md, api-conventions.md, deployment.md)
Using the /memory command to verify which memory files are loaded and diagnose inconsistent behavior across sessions
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.2 Create and configure custom slash commands and skills
subtopics: Project-scoped commands in .claude/commands/ (shared via version control) vs user-scoped commands in ~/.claude/commands/ (personal)
Skills in .claude/skills/ with SKILL.md files that support frontmatter configuration including context: fork, allowed-tools, and argument-hint
The context: fork frontmatter option for running skills in an isolated sub-agent context, preventing skill outputs from polluting the main conversation
Personal skill customization: creating personal variants in ~/.claude/skills/ with different names to avoid affecting teammates
Creating project-scoped slash commands in .claude/commands/ for team-wide availability via version control
Using context: fork to isolate skills that produce verbose output (e.g., codebase analysis) or exploratory context (e.g., brainstorming alternatives) from the main session
Configuring allowed-tools in skill frontmatter to restrict tool access during skill execution (e.g., limiting to file write operations to prevent destructive actions)
Using argument-hint frontmatter to prompt developers for required parameters when they invoke the skill without arguments
Choosing between skills (on-demand invocation for task-specific workflows) and CLAUDE.md (always-loaded universal standards)
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.3 Apply path-specific rules for conditional convention loading
subtopics: .claude/rules/ files with YAML frontmatter paths fields containing glob patterns for conditional rule activation
How path-scoped rules load only when editing matching files, reducing irrelevant context and token usage
The advantage of glob-pattern rules over directory-level CLAUDE.md files for conventions that span multiple directories (e.g., test files spread throughout a codebase)
Creating .claude/rules/ files with YAML frontmatter path scoping (e.g., paths: ["terraform/**/*"]) so rules load only when editing matching files
Using glob patterns in path-specific rules to apply conventions to files by type regardless of directory location (e.g., **/*.test.tsx for all test files)
Choosing path-specific rules over subdirectory CLAUDE.md files when conventions must apply to files spread across the codebase
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.4 Determine when to use plan mode vs direct execution
subtopics: Plan mode is designed for complex tasks involving large-scale changes, multiple valid approaches, architectural decisions, and multi-file modifications
Direct execution is appropriate for simple, well-scoped changes (e.g., adding a single validation check to one function)
Plan mode enables safe codebase exploration and design before committing to changes, preventing costly rework
The Explore subagent for isolating verbose discovery output and returning summaries to preserve main conversation context

Selecting plan mode for tasks with architectural implications (e.g., microservice restructuring, library migrations affecting 45+ files, choosing between integration approaches with different infrastructure requirements)
Selecting direct execution for well-understood changes with clear scope (e.g., a single-file bug fix with a clear stack trace, adding a date validation conditional)
Using the Explore subagent for verbose discovery phases to prevent context window exhaustion during multi-phase tasks
Combining plan mode for investigation with direct execution for implementation (e.g., planning a library migration, then executing the planned approach)
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.5 Apply iterative refinement techniques for progressive improvement
subtopics: Concrete input/output examples as the most effective way to communicate expected transformations when prose descriptions are interpreted inconsistently
Test-driven iteration: writing test suites first, then iterating by sharing test failures to guide progressive improvement
The interview pattern: having Claude ask questions to surface considerations the developer may not have anticipated before implementing
When to provide all issues in a single message (interacting problems) versus fixing them sequentially (independent problems)
Providing 2-3 concrete input/output examples to clarify transformation requirements when natural language descriptions produce inconsistent results
Writing test suites covering expected behavior, edge cases, and performance requirements before implementation, then iterating by sharing test failures
Using the interview pattern to surface design considerations (e.g., cache invalidation strategies, failure modes) before implementing solutions in unfamiliar domains
Providing specific test cases with example input and expected output to fix edge case handling (e.g., null values in migration scripts)
Addressing multiple interacting issues in a single detailed message when fixes interact, versus sequential iteration for independent issues
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 3.6 Integrate Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines
subtopics: The -p (or --print) flag for running Claude Code in non-interactive mode in automated pipelines
output-format json and --json-schema CLI flags for enforcing structured output in CI contexts
CLAUDE.md as the mechanism for providing project context (testing standards, fixture conventions, review criteria) to CI-invoked Claude Code
Session context isolation: why the same Claude session that generated code is less effective at reviewing its own changes compared to an independent review instance
Running Claude Code in CI with the -p flag to prevent interactive input hangs
Using --output-format json with --json-schema to produce machine-parseable structured findings for automated posting as inline PR comments
Including prior review findings in context when re-running reviews after new commits, instructing Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues to avoid duplicate comments
Providing existing test files in context so test generation avoids suggesting duplicate scenarios already covered by the test suite
Documenting testing standards, valuable test criteria, and available fixtures in CLAUDE.md to improve test generation quality and reduce low-value test output
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.1 Design prompts with explicit criteria to improve precision and reduce false positives
subtopics: The importance of explicit criteria over vague instructions (e.g., "flag comments only when claimed behavior contradicts actual code behavior" vs "check that comments are accurate")
How general instructions like "be conservative" or "only report high-confidence findings" fail to improve precision compared to specific categorical criteria
The impact of false positive rates on developer trust: high false positive categories undermine confidence in accurate categories
Writing specific review criteria that define which issues to report (bugs, security) versus skip (minor style, local patterns) rather than relying on confidence-based filtering
Temporarily disabling high false-positive categories to restore developer trust while improving prompts for those categories
Defining explicit severity criteria with concrete code examples for each severity level to achieve consistent classification
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.2 Apply few-shot prompting to improve output consistency and quality
subtopics: Few-shot examples as the most effective technique for achieving consistently formatted, actionable output when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results
The role of few-shot examples in demonstrating ambiguous-case handling (e.g., tool selection for ambiguous requests, branch-level test coverage gaps)
How few-shot examples enable the model to generalize judgment to novel patterns rather than matching only pre-specified cases
The effectiveness of few-shot examples for reducing hallucination in extraction tasks (e.g., handling informal measurements, varied document structures)
Creating 2-4 targeted few-shot examples for ambiguous scenarios that show reasoning for why one action was chosen over plausible alternatives
Including few-shot examples that demonstrate specific desired output format (location, issue, severity, suggested fix) to achieve consistency
Providing few-shot examples distinguishing acceptable code patterns from genuine issues to reduce false positives while enabling generalization
Using few-shot examples to demonstrate correct handling of varied document structures (inline citations vs bibliographies, methodology sections vs embedded details)
Adding few-shot examples showing correct extraction from documents with varied formats to address empty/null extraction of required fields
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.3 Enforce structured output using tool use and JSON schemas
subtopics: Tool use (tool_use) with JSON schemas as the most reliable approach for guaranteed schema-compliant structured output, eliminating JSON syntax errors

The distinction between tool_choice: "auto" (model may return text instead of calling a tool), "any" (model must call a tool but can choose which), and forced tool selection (model must call a specific named tool)
That strict JSON schemas via tool use eliminate syntax errors but do not prevent semantic errors (e.g., line items that don't sum to total, values in wrong fields)
Schema design considerations: required vs optional fields, enum fields with "other" + detail string patterns for extensible categories
Defining extraction tools with JSON schemas as input parameters and extracting structured data from the tool_use response
Setting tool_choice: "any" to guarantee structured output when multiple extraction schemas exist and the document type is unknown
Forcing a specific tool with tool_choice: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} to ensure a particular extraction runs before enrichment steps
Designing schema fields as optional (nullable) when source documents may not contain the information, preventing the model from fabricating values to satisfy required fields
Adding enum values like "unclear" for ambiguous cases and "other" + detail fields for extensible categorization
Including format normalization rules in prompts alongside strict output schemas to handle inconsistent source formatting
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.4 Implement validation, retry, and feedback loops for extraction quality
subtopics: Retry-with-error-feedback: appending specific validation errors to the prompt on retry to guide the model toward correction
The limits of retry: retries are ineffective when the required information is simply absent from the source document (vs format or structural errors)
Feedback loop design: tracking which code constructs trigger findings (detected_pattern field) to enable systematic analysis of dismissal patterns
The difference between semantic validation errors (values don't sum, wrong field placement) and schema syntax errors (eliminated by tool use)
Implementing follow-up requests that include the original document, the failed extraction, and specific validation errors for model self-correction
Identifying when retries will be ineffective (e.g., information exists only in an external document not provided) versus when they will succeed (format mismatches, structural output errors)
Adding detected_pattern fields to structured findings to enable analysis of false positive patterns when developers dismiss findings
Designing self-correction validation flows: extracting "calculated_total" alongside "stated_total" to flag discrepancies, adding "conflict_detected" booleans for inconsistent source data
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.5 Design efficient batch processing strategies
subtopics: The Message Batches API: 50% cost savings, up to 24-hour processing window, no guaranteed latency SLA
Batch processing is appropriate for non-blocking, latency-tolerant workloads (overnight reports, weekly audits, nightly test generation) and inappropriate for blocking workflows (pre-merge checks)
The batch API does not support multi-turn tool calling within a single request (cannot execute tools mid-request and return results)
custom_id fields for correlating batch request/response pairs
Matching API approach to workflow latency requirements: synchronous API for blocking pre-merge checks, batch API for overnight/weekly analysis
Calculating batch submission frequency based on SLA constraints (e.g., 4-hour windows to guarantee 30-hour SLA with 24-hour batch processing)
Handling batch failures: resubmitting only failed documents (identified by custom_id) with appropriate modifications (e.g., chunking documents that exceeded context limits)
Using prompt refinement on a sample set before batch-processing large volumes to maximize first-pass success rates and reduce iterative resubmission costs
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 4.6 Design multi-instance and multi-pass review architectures
subtopics: Self-review limitations: a model retains reasoning context from generation, making it less likely to question its own decisions in the same session
Independent review instances (without prior reasoning context) are more effective at catching subtle issues than self-review instructions or extended thinking
Multi-pass review: splitting large reviews into per-file local analysis passes plus cross-file integration passes to avoid attention dilution and contradictory findings
Using a second independent Claude instance to review generated code without the generator's reasoning context
Splitting large multi-file reviews into focused per-file passes for local issues plus separate integration passes for cross-file data flow analysis
Running verification passes where the model self-reports confidence alongside each finding to enable calibrated review routing
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 5.1 Manage conversation context to preserve critical information across long interactions
subtopics: Progressive summarization risks: condensing numerical values, percentages, dates, and customer-stated expectations into vague summaries
The "lost in the middle" effect: models reliably process information at the beginning and end of long inputs but may omit findings from middle sections
How tool results accumulate in context and consume tokens disproportionately to their relevance (e.g., 40+ fields per order lookup when only 5 are relevant)
The importance of passing complete conversation history in subsequent API requests to maintain conversational coherence
Extracting transactional facts (amounts, dates, order numbers, statuses) into a persistent "case facts" block included in each prompt, outside summarized history
Extracting and persisting structured issue data (order IDs, amounts, statuses) into a separate context layer for multi-issue sessions
Trimming verbose tool outputs to only relevant fields before they accumulate in context (e.g., keeping only return-relevant fields from order lookups)
Placing key findings summaries at the beginning of aggregated inputs and organizing detailed results with explicit section headers to mitigate position effects
Requiring subagents to include metadata (dates, source locations, methodological context) in structured outputs to support accurate downstream synthesis
Modifying upstream agents to return structured data (key facts, citations, relevance scores) instead of verbose content and reasoning chains when downstream agents have limited context budgets
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 5.2 Design effective escalation and ambiguity resolution patterns
subtopics: Appropriate escalation triggers: customer requests for a human, policy exceptions/gaps (not just complex cases), and inability to make meaningful progress
The distinction between escalating immediately when a customer explicitly demands it versus offering to resolve when the issue is straightforward
Why sentiment-based escalation and self-reported confidence scores are unreliable proxies for actual case complexity
How multiple customer matches require clarification (requesting additional identifiers) rather than heuristic selection
Adding explicit escalation criteria with few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating when to escalate versus resolve autonomously
Honoring explicit customer requests for human agents immediately without first attempting investigation
Acknowledging frustration while offering resolution when the issue is within the agent's capability, escalating only if the customer reiterates their preference
Escalating when policy is ambiguous or silent on the customer's specific request (e.g., competitor price matching when policy only addresses own-site adjustments)
Instructing the agent to ask for additional identifiers when tool results return multiple matches, rather than selecting based on heuristics
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 5.3 Implement error propagation strategies across multi-agent systems
subtopics: Structured error context (failure type, attempted query, partial results, alternative approaches) as enabling intelligent coordinator recovery decisions
The distinction between access failures (timeouts needing retry decisions) and valid empty results (successful queries with no matches)
Why generic error statuses ("search unavailable") hide valuable context from the coordinator
Why silently suppressing errors (returning empty results as success) or terminating entire workflows on single failures are both anti-patterns
Returning structured error context including failure type, what was attempted, partial results, and potential alternatives to enable coordinator recovery
Distinguishing access failures from valid empty results in error reporting so the coordinator can make appropriate decisions
Having subagents implement local recovery for transient failures and only propagate errors they cannot resolve, including what was attempted and partial results
Structuring synthesis output with coverage annotations indicating which findings are well-supported versus which topic areas have gaps due to unavailable sources
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 5.4 Manage context effectively in large codebase exploration
subtopics: Context degradation in extended sessions: models start giving inconsistent answers and referencing "typical patterns" rather than specific classes discovered earlier
The role of scratchpad files for persisting key findings across context boundaries
Subagent delegation for isolating verbose exploration output while the main agent coordinates high-level understanding
Structured state persistence for crash recovery: each agent exports state to a known location, and the coordinator loads a manifest on resume
Spawning subagents to investigate specific questions (e.g., "find all test files," "trace refund flow dependencies") while the main agent preserves high-level coordination
Having agents maintain scratchpad files recording key findings, referencing them for subsequent questions to counteract context degradation
Summarizing key findings from one exploration phase before spawning sub-agents for the next phase, injecting summaries into initial context
Designing crash recovery using structured agent state exports (manifests) that the coordinator loads on resume and injects into agent prompts
Using /compact to reduce context usage during extended exploration sessions when context fills with verbose discovery output

units.title: 5.5 Design human review workflows and confidence calibration
subtopics: The risk that aggregate accuracy metrics (e.g., 97% overall) may mask poor performance on specific document types or fields
Stratified random sampling for measuring error rates in high-confidence extractions and detecting novel error patterns
Field-level confidence scores calibrated using labeled validation sets for routing review attention
The importance of validating accuracy by document type and field segment before automating high-confidence extractions
Implementing stratified random sampling of high-confidence extractions for ongoing error rate measurement and novel pattern detection
Analyzing accuracy by document type and field to verify consistent performance across all segments before reducing human review
Having models output field-level confidence scores, then calibrating review thresholds using labeled validation sets
Routing extractions with low model confidence or ambiguous/contradictory source documents to human review, prioritizing limited reviewer capacity
units.enhancementInstructions: Frame for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam: emphasise production tradeoffs and architectural judgment; include one realistic scenario and 2–3 exam-style multiple-choice questions with explained answers.

units.title: 5.6 Preserve information provenance and handle uncertainty in multi-source synthesis
subtopics: How source attribution is lost during summarization steps when findings are compressed without preserving claim-source mappings
The importance of structured claim-source mappings that the synthesis agent must preserve and merge when combining findings
How to handle conflicting statistics from credible sources: annotating conflicts with source attribution rather than arbitrarily selecting one value
Temporal data: requiring publication/collection dates in structured outputs to prevent temporal differences from being misinterpreted as contradictions
Requiring subagents to output structured claim-source mappings (source URLs, document names, relevant excerpts) that downstream agents preserve through synthesis
Structuring reports with explicit sections distinguishing well-established findings from contested ones, preserving original source characterizations and methodological context
Completing document analysis with conflicting values included and explicitly annotated, letting the coordinator decide how to reconcile before passing to synthesis
Requiring subagents to include publication or data collection dates in structured outputs to enable correct temporal interpretation

Rendering different content types appropriately in synthesis outputs—financial data as tables, news as prose, technical findings as structured lists—rather than converting everything to a uniform format

ts-1-1.title: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution

lesson.topic: 1.1 Design and implement agentic loops for autonomous task execution — covering: The agentic loop lifecycle: sending requests to Claude, inspecting stop_reason ("tool_use" vs "end_turn"), executing requested tools, and returning results for the next iteration, How tool results are appended to conversation history so the model can reason about the next action, The distinction between model-driven decision-making (Claude reasons about which tool to call next based on context) and pre-configured decision trees or tool sequences, Implementing agentic loop control flow that continues when stop_reason is "tool_use" and terminates when stop_reason is "end_turn", Adding tool results to conversation context between iterations so the model can incorporate new information into its reasoning, Avoiding anti-patterns such as parsing natural language signals to determine loop termination, setting arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism, or checking for assistant text content as a completion indicator
lesson.synopsis: This lesson teaches working professionals how to design and implement production-grade agentic loops with Claude, covering the full lifecycle from sending a request to inspecting `stop_reason`, executing tools, and feeding results back into conversation history. It draws a sharp architectural distinction between model-driven decision-making and hard-coded decision trees, and equips learners to avoid the anti-patterns that most commonly cause agentic systems to fail or behave unpredictably. The content is framed for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam, emphasising production tradeoffs and architectural judgment throughout.
learning_objectives: Explain the full agentic loop lifecycle — request, stop_reason inspection, tool execution, and result injection — and justify each step's role in autonomous task execution.
Implement correct control flow that continues iteration on `stop_reason: tool_use` and terminates cleanly on `stop_reason: end_turn`.
Distinguish model-driven decision-making from pre-configured tool sequences and articulate when each architecture is appropriate.
Construct well-formed conversation history that appends tool results so Claude can incorporate new information into subsequent reasoning steps.
Identify and refactor the three primary agentic loop anti-patterns: natural-language termination signals, arbitrary iteration caps, and assistant-text completion checks.

sections.heading: The Agentic Loop at a Glance
sections.body_markdown:
An agentic loop is the repeating request–respond–act–return cycle that lets Claude autonomously complete multi-step tasks without human intervention between steps. Understanding this cycle at the architectural level — not just the code level — is the foundation of every production agentic system.

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 320 160" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Animated agentic loop cycle">
  <!-- Track -->
  <ellipse cx="160" cy="80" rx="120" ry="55" fill="none" stroke="#334155" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 5"/>
  <!-- Labels at cardinal points -->
  <text x="160" y="18" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1" font-family="sans-serif">Send Request</text>
  <text x="292" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b" font-family="sans-serif">Inspect</text>
  <text x="293" y="96" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#f59e0b" font-family="sans-serif">stop_reason</text>
  <text x="160" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#22c55e" font-family="sans-serif">Execute Tool</text>
  <text x="4" y="84" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1" font-family="sans-serif">Append</text>
  <text x="4" y="96" text-anchor="start" font-size="10" fill="#6366f1" font-family="sans-serif">Result</text>
  <!-- Orbiting dot -->
  <g>
    <circle r="9" fill="#6366f1"/>
    <animateMotion dur="5s" repeatCount="indefinite"
      path="M 280 80 A 120 55 0 1 1 40 80 A 120 55 0 1 1 280 80"/>
  </g>
</svg>
```

The four phases repeat until Claude signals it is done:

| Phase | What happens | Key artifact |
|:------|:-------------|:-------------|
| **Send Request** | Your orchestrator calls the Messages API with the current conversation history and tool definitions | `POST /v1/messages` |
| **Inspect `stop_reason`** | You read the `stop_reason` field on the response — `"tool_use"` means Claude wants to call a tool; `"end_turn"` means it is finished | `response.stop_reason` |
| **Execute Tool** | Your code runs the requested tool (database query, API call, file read, etc.) and captures the result | Tool output |
| **Append Result** | You add both the assistant's tool-use block and your `tool_result` block to the conversation history, then loop back to Phase 1 | Updated `messages[]` |

The loop terminates when `stop_reason` is `"end_turn"`. Every other design decision in this lesson flows from this simple state machine.

> The art of progress is to preserve order amid change, and to preserve change amid order.
> — Alfred North Whitehead

That balance — structured control flow around a flexible reasoning engine — is exactly what a well-designed agentic loop achieves.

sections.heading: The Agentic Loop Lifecycle in Detail
sections.body_markdown:
Each phase of the loop has precise semantics. Getting them right is the difference between a system that works reliably in production and one that fails on edge cases.

### Phase 1 — Sending the Request

Every iteration sends the *full* conversation history plus the tool schema. Claude has no persistent memory between API calls; the history you send is its entire world.

```python
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def run_agentic_loop(user_message: str, tools: list[dict]) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-opus-4-5",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages
        )

        # Phase 2 — inspect stop_reason
        if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            # Extract the final text answer
            return next(
                block.text
                for block in response.content
                if block.type == "text"
            )

        if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
            # Phase 3 — execute tools
            tool_results = []
            for block in response.content:
                if block.type == "tool_use":
                    result = dispatch_tool(block.name, block.input)
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": result
                    })

            # Phase 4 — append to history
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
            # Loop continues
```

### Phase 2 — Inspecting `stop_reason`

The `stop_reason` field is the **only authoritative signal** for loop control. The two values you must handle are:

| `stop_reason` | Meaning | Correct action |
|:--------------|:--------|:---------------|
| `"tool_use"` | Claude has decided to call one or more tools | Execute the tools, append results, iterate |
| `"end_turn"` | Claude has finished reasoning and produced a final answer | Terminate the loop, return the answer |
| `"max_tokens"` | The response was truncated | Handle as an error or extend `max_tokens` |
| `"stop_sequence"` | A custom stop sequence was hit | Treat as termination; inspect context |

### Phase 3 — Executing Tools

Tool execution is **your code**, not Claude's. Claude emits a `tool_use` content block containing:
- `id` — a unique identifier you must echo back
- `name` — the tool name matching your schema
- `input` — a JSON object of arguments

Your `dispatch_tool` function routes by name and returns a string or structured result. Keep this function pure and testable — it is the seam between the AI layer and your business logic.

### Phase 4 — Appending Results

The result must be appended as a `user`-role message containing a `tool_result` block. The `tool_use_id` field links the result back to the specific tool call, which is critical when Claude issues **parallel tool calls** (multiple `tool_use` blocks in a single response). You must return *all* results before the next API call.

```python
# Correct: all results in one user message
messages.append({"role": "user", "content": [
    {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_01", "content": "42"},
    {"type": "tool_result", "tool_use_id": "toolu_02", "content": "Paris"}
]})

# WRONG: splitting results across two user messages
# This violates the alternating user/assistant turn structure
```

**Edge case — tool errors:** If a tool raises an exception, return the error as a `tool_result` with `"is_error": true`. Claude will read the error message and decide whether to retry with different arguments, use a fallback tool, or report failure to the user. Never silently swallow tool errors.

sections.heading: Conversation History as Reasoning Memory
sections.body_markdown:
Claude has no side-channel memory. The `messages` array you pass on each API call is the complete context Claude uses to reason about what to do next. Treating history management as a first-class architectural concern — not an afterthought — is what separates robust agentic systems from fragile ones.

### The Alternating Turn Structure

The Messages API enforces a strict alternating `user` / `assistant` pattern. Each iteration of the agentic loop adds exactly two turns:

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant C as Claude API
    O->>C: messages=[user: task]
    C-->>O: assistant: [tool_use block] | stop_reason=tool_use
    Note over O: Execute tool, get result
    O->>C: messages=[user: task, assistant: [tool_use], user: [tool_result]]
    C-->>O: assistant: [text block] | stop_reason=end_turn
    Note over O: Loop terminates
```

After $n$ tool-use iterations, the history has $2n + 1$ turns (the original user message plus $n$ assistant/user pairs).

### Why History Integrity Matters

If you truncate, reorder, or omit turns, Claude loses the chain of reasoning that led to the current state. Concretely:

- **Omitting a `tool_result`** causes an API validation error — Claude cannot proceed without knowing what the tool returned.
- **Truncating mid-task** causes Claude to re-request tools it already called, creating infinite loops or duplicate side effects.
- **Reordering turns** breaks causal reasoning — Claude may contradict earlier conclusions.

### Context Window Management

For long-running tasks, the history can grow to fill the context window ($\approx 200{,}000$ tokens for Claude's largest models). Production strategies include:

| Strategy | Tradeoff | Best for |
|:---------|:---------|:---------|
| **Summarise completed sub-tasks** | Loses fine-grained detail | Long research tasks |
| **Sliding window** (keep last $k$ turns) | May lose early constraints | Conversational agents |
| **Structured state object** (inject as system prompt) | Requires explicit state design | Workflow automation |
| **Full history** (no truncation) | Highest token cost | Short, high-stakes tasks |

The architectural judgment call: truncation strategies trade **cost and latency** against **reasoning fidelity**. For tasks where early context contains hard constraints (e.g., "never modify production data"), full history or a structured state object is safer than a sliding window.

sections.heading: Model-Driven Decision-Making vs. Pre-Configured Tool Sequences
sections.body_markdown:
One of the most consequential architectural decisions in agentic system design is whether Claude chooses the next tool dynamically or whether your orchestrator pre-determines the sequence. These are fundamentally different paradigms with different tradeoffs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Task]:::concept --> B{Architecture Choice}:::decision
    B -->|Dynamic| C[Claude reasons\nwhich tool next]:::process
    B -->|Pre-configured| D[Orchestrator\nfollows fixed DAG]:::process
    C --> E[Flexible, handles\nunexpected states]:::success
    C --> F[Harder to audit,\ncost unpredictable]:::warn
    D --> G[Auditable, cost\npredictable]:::success
    D --> H[Brittle when\ntask varies]:::warn
```

### Model-Driven Decision-Making

In a model-driven loop, Claude reads the current conversation history — including all prior tool results — and decides which tool to call next (or whether to stop). The orchestrator's job is purely mechanical: execute whatever Claude requests.

**When to use it:**
- Tasks with variable structure (e.g., open-ended research, debugging)
- Tasks where the next step depends on the *content* of prior results
- Rapid prototyping where the full task graph is not yet known

**Production tradeoffs:**
- ✅ Handles unexpected intermediate states gracefully
- ✅ Requires no upfront task decomposition
- ⚠️ Token cost per task is harder to bound
- ⚠️ Harder to unit-test individual decision branches
- ⚠️ Requires careful tool schema design to prevent hallucinated tool names

### Pre-Configured Decision Trees / Tool Sequences

In a pre-configured system, the orchestrator drives a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of tool calls. Claude may still be used for individual steps (e.g., to parse a result or generate a sub-query), but the *routing logic* lives in your code.

```python
# Pre-configured sequence — orchestrator drives the flow
def run_etl_pipeline(source_id: str):
    raw = extract_tool(source_id)          # Step 1: always
    validated = validate_tool(raw)         # Step 2: always
    if validated["has_anomalies"]:
        report = anomaly_report_tool(validated)  # Step 3a: conditional
    else:
        report = load_tool(validated)            # Step 3b: conditional
    return report
```

**When to use it:**
- Well-understood, repeatable workflows (ETL, document processing)
- Compliance-sensitive tasks requiring an audit trail
- Cost-sensitive production systems where token usage must be bounded

### The Hybrid Pattern

Most production systems use a hybrid: a pre-configured outer workflow with model-driven sub-agents for steps that require flexible reasoning. For example, an ETL pipeline might have fixed extract/load steps but use a model-driven loop for the transform step when the schema is unknown.

| Dimension | Model-Driven | Pre-Configured | Hybrid |
|:----------|:-------------|:---------------|:-------|
| Flexibility | High | Low | Medium |
| Cost predictability | Low | High | Medium |
| Auditability | Low | High | Medium |
| Dev time for new tasks | Low | High | Medium |
| Failure mode | Unexpected tool calls | Brittle branching | Bounded by outer DAG |

sections.heading: Implementing Correct Control Flow
sections.body_markdown:
The control flow of an agentic loop is simple in principle but easy to get wrong under production conditions. This section walks through a complete, production-hardened implementation and explains every decision.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Build initial messages]:::process --> B[Call Messages API]:::process
    B --> C{stop_reason?}:::decision
    C -->|end_turn| D[Extract final text\nReturn to caller]:::success
    C -->|tool_use| E[Collect all tool_use\nblocks from response]:::process
    E --> F[Execute each tool\nin parallel or serial]:::process
    F --> G{Any tool\nerror?}:::decision
    G -->|yes| H[Append error\ntool_result with\nis_error: true]:::warn
    G -->|no| I[Append success\ntool_result]:::process
    H --> J[Append assistant turn\n+ user tool_results]:::process
    I --> J
    J --> B
    C -->|max_tokens| K[Raise ContextError\nor extend budget]:::warn
    C -->|stop_sequence| L[Treat as end_turn\nLog for review]:::warn
```

### Production-Hardened Implementation

```python
import anthropic
from typing import Callable

class AgentLoopError(Exception):
    pass

def run_agent(
    user_message: str,
    tools: list[dict],
    tool_executor: Callable[[str, dict], str],
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens: int = 4096,
) -> str:
    """
    Runs a model-driven agentic loop until stop_reason is 'end_turn'.
    Returns the final text response.
    """
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_message}]

    while True:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model=model,
            max_tokens=max_tokens,
            tools=tools,
            messages=messages,
        )

        stop_reason = response.stop_reason

        # --- Terminal conditions ---
        if stop_reason == "end_turn":
            text_blocks = [b for b in response.content if b.type == "text"]
            if not text_blocks:
                raise AgentLoopError("end_turn with no text content")
            return text_blocks[-1].text

        if stop_reason == "max_tokens":
            raise AgentLoopError(
                f"Response truncated at {max_tokens} tokens. "
                "Increase max_tokens or summarise history."
            )

        # --- Tool-use iteration ---
        if stop_reason == "tool_use":
            tool_use_blocks = [
                b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use"
            ]

            tool_results = []
            for block in tool_use_blocks:
                try:
                    result_content = tool_executor(block.name, block.input)
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": result_content,
                    })
                except Exception as exc:
                    # Surface the error to Claude; let it decide how to proceed
                    tool_results.append({
                        "type": "tool_result",
                        "tool_use_id": block.id,
                        "content": f"Error: {exc}",
                        "is_error": True,
                    })

            # Append assistant turn, then user turn with all results
            messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response.content})
            messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})
            continue  # Next iteration

        # Unexpected stop_reason — fail loudly
        raise AgentLoopError(f"Unhandled stop_reason: {stop_reason!r}")
```

### Key Design Decisions Explained

**Why `response.content` (not just text) is appended as the assistant turn:** The assistant turn must include the `tool_use` blocks, not just any text. If you strip them out, the subsequent `tool_result` blocks will have no matching `tool_use_id` and the API will reject the request.

**Why all tool results go in a single user message:** The API requires that a `tool_result` block immediately follows the assistant turn that contained the corresponding `tool_use` block. Splitting results across multiple user messages breaks the alternating turn structure.

**Why `max_tokens` truncation raises an error:** A truncated response may contain a partial `tool_use` block. Silently continuing would corrupt the conversation history. Fail loudly and let the caller decide whether to retry with a larger budget or summarise history first.

sections.heading: Anti-Patterns to Avoid
sections.body_markdown:
Three anti-patterns appear repeatedly in production agentic systems. Each one looks reasonable at first glance but causes subtle, hard-to-debug failures.

### Anti-Pattern 1 — Parsing Natural Language for Termination

**What it looks like:**
```python
# ANTI-PATTERN: parsing text to decide when to stop
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "text":
        if "task complete" in block.text.lower() or \
           "i have finished" in block.text.lower():
            return block.text  # ← unreliable
```

**Why it fails:** Claude's phrasing is non-deterministic. It may say "I've completed the analysis" mid-task while still planning to call another tool. It may also complete a task without using any completion phrase. The `stop_reason` field is the authoritative, structured signal — use it exclusively.

**The fix:** Check `response.stop_reason == "end_turn"` and nothing else.

---

### Anti-Pattern 2 — Arbitrary Iteration Caps as the Primary Stopping Mechanism

**What it looks like:**
```python
# ANTI-PATTERN: iteration cap as the main exit condition
for i in range(10):  # ← magic number
    response = client.messages.create(...)
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        break
    # ... execute tools ...
else:
    return "Sorry, I couldn't complete the task."  # silent failure
```

**Why it fails:** The cap is either too low (cutting off legitimate multi-step tasks) or too high (masking infinite loops caused by bugs). When the cap is hit, the caller receives a degraded response with no indication of what went wrong.

**The fix:** Use `stop_reason` as the primary exit. Add a cap only as a **safety circuit breaker** that raises a named exception with diagnostic context:

```python
MAX_ITERATIONS = 50  # safety ceiling, not the expected exit

for iteration in range(MAX_ITERATIONS):
    response = client.messages.create(...)
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        return extract_text(response)
    if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
        messages = append_tool_results(messages, response)
        continue
    handle_unexpected_stop(response.stop_reason)

raise AgentLoopError(
    f"Safety cap reached after {MAX_ITERATIONS} iterations. "
    f"Last stop_reason: {response.stop_reason!r}. "
    f"Last tool called: {last_tool_name!r}."
)
```

**Production tradeoff:** Set the cap high enough that legitimate tasks never hit it, but low enough that runaway loops are caught quickly. For most tasks, 20–50 iterations is a reasonable ceiling; for complex research agents, 100–200 may be appropriate.

---

### Anti-Pattern 3 — Checking for Assistant Text as a Completion Indicator

**What it looks like:**
```python
# ANTI-PATTERN: presence of text content signals completion
for block in response.content:
    if block.type == "text" and len(block.text) > 0:
        return block.text  # ← wrong: text can appear alongside tool_use
```

**Why it fails:** Claude often emits a `text` block *alongside* a `tool_use` block in the same response — for example, to explain its reasoning before calling a tool. Returning on the first text block would terminate the loop prematurely, before the tool has been called.

**The fix:** Never use the presence or absence of text content to drive loop control. Only `stop_reason` determines whether to continue or terminate.

---

### Anti-Pattern Summary

| Anti-Pattern | Root Cause | Failure Mode | Fix |
|:-------------|:-----------|:-------------|:----|
| NL termination parsing | Treating model output as structured signal | Non-deterministic early/late exit | Use `stop_reason` exclusively |
| Iteration cap as primary exit | Distrust of model's own termination signal | Silent truncation or masked loops | Cap = safety circuit breaker only |
| Text-presence check | Misunderstanding mixed content blocks | Premature loop exit | Ignore text content for control flow |

sections.heading: Realistic Scenario: Autonomous Financial Research Agent
sections.body_markdown:
This scenario illustrates how the concepts above interact in a realistic production context, and surfaces the architectural judgment calls you will face on the exam.

### Scenario Description

Your team is building an autonomous agent that answers questions like: *"What were the top three revenue drivers for Acme Corp last quarter, and how do they compare to the prior quarter?"*

The agent has access to four tools:

| Tool | Description | Latency | Cost |
|:-----|:------------|:--------|-----:|
| `search_filings` | Full-text search over SEC filings | ~800 ms | Low |
| `get_financial_table` | Returns a structured table from a filing | ~200 ms | Low |
| `calculate_yoy_change` | Computes year-over-year delta | ~10 ms | Negligible |
| `generate_summary` | Calls a secondary LLM for prose summarisation | ~2,000 ms | High |

### A Typical Execution Trace

```
Iteration 1:
  Claude calls: search_filings(query="Acme Corp Q3 revenue drivers")
  stop_reason: tool_use

Iteration 2:
  Claude receives: [list of 3 matching filing IDs]
  Claude calls: get_financial_table(filing_id="2024-Q3", section="revenue")
             AND get_financial_table(filing_id="2024-Q2", section="revenue")
  stop_reason: tool_use  ← parallel tool calls

Iteration 3:
  Claude receives: [Q3 revenue table, Q2 revenue table]
  Claude calls: calculate_yoy_change(q3_data=..., q2_data=...)
  stop_reason: tool_use

Iteration 4:
  Claude receives: [{"segment": "Cloud", "change": "+18%"}, ...]
  Claude synthesises the answer directly (skips generate_summary)
  stop_reason: end_turn  ← loop terminates
```

### Architectural Judgment Calls

**1. Should `generate_summary` be in the tool schema?**
Including it gives Claude the option to use it when the answer is complex. Excluding it forces Claude to synthesise in its own response, saving latency and cost. For this use case, the model's native synthesis is sufficient — omit the tool and reduce the attack surface.

**2. Should the two `get_financial_table` calls in Iteration 2 be parallelised?**
Yes. Claude will emit both `tool_use` blocks in a single response. Your orchestrator should execute them concurrently (e.g., with `asyncio.gather`) and return both results in a single user message. This halves the latency of that iteration.

**3. What if `search_filings` returns zero results?**
Claude will receive an empty list as the `tool_result`. A well-designed agent will then either try a broader query or report that no filings were found — without your orchestrator needing to handle this case explicitly. This is the key advantage of model-driven decision-making over a pre-configured sequence.

**4. What is the right iteration cap for this agent?**
For a four-tool research task, 10 iterations is a generous ceiling. Set it to 20 to allow for retries on tool errors, and raise a named `ResearchAgentCapExceeded` exception with the full message history attached for post-mortem debugging.

---

## Exam-Style Questions

### Question 1

An agentic loop calls the Messages API and receives a response where `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"`. The response's `content` array contains one `text` block (Claude explaining its plan) and two `tool_use` blocks. What is the correct next action?

**A.** Return the text block to the user as a partial answer, then execute the two tools and continue the loop.

**B.** Execute both tools, collect their results, append the full `response.content` as an assistant turn and both results as a single user turn, then call the API again.

**C.** Execute only the first `tool_use` block, append its result, and call the API again; handle the second tool in the next iteration.

**D.** Discard the text block, execute both tools, and append only the `tool_result` blocks as a new user message without appending the assistant turn.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* When `stop_reason` is `"tool_use"`, you must execute **all** tool-use blocks in the response (not just the first), append the **full** `response.content` (including the text block) as the assistant turn, and return **all** tool results in a **single** user message. Option A incorrectly returns a partial answer. Option C incorrectly splits tool execution across iterations, which violates the requirement to return all results before the next API call. Option D omits the assistant turn, which breaks the alternating turn structure and will cause an API validation error.

---

### Question 2

A production agentic loop has been running reliably for weeks. Suddenly, it starts terminating after exactly 15 iterations on complex tasks, returning incomplete answers. The loop was implemented with `for i in range(15)` as the outer loop and `stop_reason == "end_turn"` as the break condition. What is the most likely root cause and the correct fix?

**A.** The model changed its behaviour and now requires more iterations. Increase the range to 30.

**B.** The iteration cap is functioning as the primary exit mechanism for tasks that legitimately require more than 15 steps. Replace the `for` loop with a `while True` loop, use `stop_reason` as the sole exit condition, and add a high-ceiling cap (e.g., 50) that raises a named exception with diagnostic context.

**C.** The `stop_reason` check is unreliable. Replace it with a check for the presence of a text block in `response.content`.

**D.** The context window is being exceeded. Reduce `max_tokens` to prevent truncation.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* The iteration cap was set too low and is now acting as the primary exit for tasks that require more than 15 tool calls. The correct fix is to make `stop_reason == "end_turn"` the sole normal exit condition and demote the cap to a safety circuit breaker. Option A treats the symptom, not the cause, and will recur as task complexity grows. Option C introduces Anti-Pattern 3 — text-presence checks are unreliable for loop control. Option D conflates `max_tokens` (response length) with iteration count.

---

### Question 3

You are designing an agentic system for a compliance-sensitive document processing workflow. Every document must go through exactly three steps — extract, validate, archive — in that order, with no variation. Which architecture is most appropriate?

**A.** A model-driven agentic loop with all three tools in the schema, relying on Claude to call them in the correct order.

**B.** A pre-configured orchestrator that calls each tool in sequence, using Claude only for the content-level reasoning within each step (e.g., extracting fields from unstructured text).

**C.** A model-driven loop with a system prompt instructing Claude to always call tools in the order: extract → validate → archive.

**D.** A model-driven loop with an iteration cap of 3 to enforce the three-step sequence.

**Correct answer: B**

*Explanation:* When the workflow is fixed, compliance-sensitive, and must be auditable, a pre-configured orchestrator is the correct choice. It guarantees the sequence, produces a deterministic audit trail, and does not rely on the model to follow instructions correctly every time. Option A introduces unnecessary non-determinism — Claude *might* call tools out of order. Option C relies on prompt-level instruction following, which is not a compliance-grade guarantee. Option D misuses the iteration cap as a workflow enforcement mechanism, which is both an anti-pattern and fragile (a tool error would consume an iteration and break the sequence).

key_takeaways: The `stop_reason` field is the **only authoritative signal** for agentic loop control: continue on `"tool_use"`, terminate on `"end_turn"`, and raise named errors on `"max_tokens"` or unexpected values.

Conversation history is Claude's entire memory — append both the assistant's full `response.content` (including `tool_use` blocks) and all `tool_result` blocks before the next API call, or the model's reasoning will be corrupted.

Model-driven decision-making maximises flexibility for variable tasks; pre-configured tool sequences maximise auditability and cost predictability for fixed workflows — choose based on task structure, not convenience.
All tool results from a single response must be returned in a **single user message** before the next API call; splitting them across messages breaks the alternating turn structure.
Iteration caps belong in every production loop as safety circuit breakers that raise diagnostic exceptions — but they must never be the primary exit mechanism.

The three canonical anti-patterns — NL termination parsing, iteration caps as primary exits, and text-presence checks — all share the same root cause: using unreliable heuristics instead of the structured `stop_reason` signal.
further_reading: Multi-agent orchestration patterns: how to compose multiple agentic loops into hierarchical or parallel agent networks, including supervisor–worker and map–reduce topologies.
Tool schema design for agentic systems: writing precise JSON Schema definitions that minimise hallucinated tool calls and guide Claude toward correct argument construction.
Context window management strategies: summarisation, structured state injection, and retrieval-augmented memory for long-running agents that exceed a single context window.
Error handling and retry strategies in agentic loops: exponential backoff, idempotency guarantees for tool side effects, and graceful degradation when tools are unavailable.
Observability for agentic systems: tracing individual iterations, logging tool call/result pairs, and building dashboards that surface runaway loops before they reach production users.

ts-1-2.title: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns

lesson.topic: 1.2 Orchestrate multi-agent systems with coordinator-subagent patterns — covering: Hub-and-spoke architecture where a coordinator agent manages all inter-subagent communication, error handling, and information routing, How subagents operate with isolated context—they do not inherit the coordinator's conversation history automatically, The role of the coordinator in task decomposition, delegation, result aggregation, and deciding which subagents to invoke based on query complexity, Risks of overly narrow task decomposition by the coordinator, leading to incomplete coverage of broad research topics, Designing coordinator agents that analyze query requirements and dynamically select which subagents to invoke rather than always routing through the full pipeline, Partitioning research scope across subagents to minimize duplication (e.g., assigning distinct subtopics or source types to each agent), Implementing iterative refinement loops where the coordinator evaluates synthesis output for gaps, re-delegates to search and analysis subagents with targeted queries, and re-invokes synthesis until coverage is sufficient, Routing all subagent communication through the coordinator for observability, consistent error handling, and controlled information flow
lesson.synopsis: This lesson examines the coordinator-subagent pattern as the primary architectural primitive for production multi-agent systems built on Claude. You will learn how a hub-and-spoke coordinator manages task decomposition, context isolation, scope partitioning, and iterative refinement while maintaining full observability over every subagent interaction. The lesson is framed for the Claude Certified Architect – Foundations exam, emphasising production tradeoffs and the architectural judgment required to design systems that are both capable and operationally sound.
learning_objectives: Explain the hub-and-spoke coordinator-subagent architecture and articulate why routing all communication through the coordinator improves observability and error handling.
Design a coordinator agent that dynamically selects which subagents to invoke based on query complexity rather than always executing a fixed pipeline.
Identify the risks of overly narrow task decomposition and apply scope-partitioning strategies to minimise duplication and coverage gaps.
Implement iterative refinement loops in which the coordinator evaluates synthesis output, detects gaps, and re-delegates targeted queries until coverage is sufficient.
Evaluate production tradeoffs — latency, cost, context budget, and failure blast radius — when choosing between shallow and deep coordinator-subagent topologies.

sections.heading: 1. Hub-and-Spoke Architecture: The Coordinator as Control Plane
sections.body_markdown:
The coordinator agent is the single point of authority in a multi-agent system — it owns every decision about which subagent runs, when it runs, and what information it receives.
